title: Content
scan: What you will learn: How content moves from source to live page — through DAM ingestion, approval gates, localization pipelines, and sitemap registration — and where that journey breaks
Why it matters: Broken content workflows are the single most common cause of go-live delays on enterprise builds; fixing them post-Sprint 5 costs three to five times what early modelling costs

Localization strategy is an architecture decision first — machine-translate-first and human-first require fundamentally different DAM folder structures, rendition naming conventions, and replication rules
The XML sitemap is a contract between SEO, Content, and Engineering — it defines what exists, what is indexable, and what the CMS must produce on every publish event
Content velocity is measurable: if an author needs more than 3 clicks to publish a page, the authoring model is wrong and throughput will be the constraint every release

blocks.html: ontent is everything the platform manages, displays, stores or publishes. The architect's leverage here isn't in writing copy — it's in the model and the workflow. Get the structure right and every release flows; get it wrong and every release becomes a fire-fight. Six discipline areas decide whether a content programme scales.

items: ↳ Rejected at any gate returns to Draft · Gate owners must be named individuals, not teams · each arrow carries an SLA

blocks.html: DAM folder taxonomy is an architecture decision, not a filing preference. The folder structure chosen in Discovery drives rendition paths, IAM permission groups, CDN cache-key patterns, and AEM replication rules. A brand-first hierarchy (/brand/campaign/locale) and a locale-first hierarchy (/locale/brand/campaign) produce entirely different CDN invalidation scopes and different asset-sharing rules across markets. Getting this wrong in week 2 costs weeks to unwind in Sprint 8 — after assets are referenced in hundreds of content fragments and replication agents are already configured.

Teams that skip approval workflow design before CMS configuration consistently ship pages that have never passed legal review. The approval topology — who approves, in what order, with what SLA, with what escalation path — must be modelled as a swimlane diagram before the first workflow is configured in the CMS. Retrofitting approval gates after a compliance incident means touching live workflow definitions, retraining authors mid-project, and auditing every piece of content published in the gap. Design the topology first; wire it second.

DEPT® field pattern: use a single Content Ready flag in Workfront or Jira that gates both legal sign-off and DAM final-rendition approval simultaneously. Two-track sign-off — legal marks approved while the DAM team is still processing final renditions, or vice versa — is the most common cause of last-minute go-live holds. One flag, two conditions, one gate. Make the flag unset-able only by a named release coordinator, not by either track independently. This eliminates the "legal said yes but the image isn't in the DAM yet" class of incident entirely.
Locale isn't language. en-IN and en-US share a language but differ in spelling, currency, regulation and cultural reference. Plan locales as markets, not as translations.

items: Translation memory — reuse of previously translated strings cuts cost and keeps tone consistent across releases.
In-country review — a local team checks the translation in real context, not as a spreadsheet of strings.
Tools — AEM multi-site manager, Smartling, Lokalise, Phrase. AI-MT is now strong enough for first pass; humans still needed for tone, idiom and regulated content.

blocks.html: "Sitemap" means two different things — and the architect cares about both:

cards.title: IA sitemap
cards.body: The conceptual site structure — page types, hierarchy, navigation. Drives the content model: what types exist, what attributes each carries.

cards.title: XML sitemap
cards.body: The file submitted to search engines for crawling. Auto-generated from the live site; updated as pages are added or retired.

blocks.html: The IA sitemap should live next to the design system, not in a stale slide deck. Without it, authors invent structure ad-hoc and the model fractures within two quarters.
For every content type, three questions: where does it come from, who provides it, in what format. Answers belong in the discovery doc, not in someone's head.

cards.title: Source
cards.body: Customer-authored · agency-created · syndicated feed · user-generated · external partner.

cards.title: Collection mechanism
cards.body: Portal upload · shared drive · intake form · API feed · in-CMS authoring.

cards.title: Volume planning
cards.body: How much per page type · growth rate · seasonal spikes · what fails at 10x.

items: ↳ A real example: product images from a PIM feed, legal copy from in-house counsel via SharePoint, campaign creative from an external agency via Frame.io, UGC from on-site form — same site, four pipelines.

blocks.html: Three roles for every piece of content — and they are rarely the same person: creator → approver → publisher. Real-world workflows add stages (editorial, legal, brand, final) and gates.

items: Named owners at every stage — not "the legal team." A person.
SLA on each gate — 24h, 48h, 5 days. Anything unbounded becomes the bottleneck.
Audit trail — who approved what, when, why. Compliance gold; also useful when production blames editorial.
Escalation — when stuck for more than the SLA, the named escalator decides.
Tooling — AEM workflows · Workfront · Jira approval transitions · custom gates.

blocks.html: Assets without governance become legal risk, brand drift, and search-by-vibe. Five disciplines keep a DAM honest:

cards.title: DAM structure
cards.body: Folder hierarchy that mirrors business reality — by brand, by campaign, by media type, by locale. One source of truth; no parallel assets-final-v3 shadow folders.

cards.title: Tags & taxonomy
cards.body: A controlled, hierarchical vocabulary (Vehicle > SUV > Compact). Free tags supplement findability, never replace structure. Periodic audits to dedupe synonyms and retire dead tags.

cards.title: Metadata management
cards.body: Required fields enforced at upload — no upload without alt-text, usage rights, expiry, photographer, model release. Standard schemas: IPTC, XMP, Dublin Core.

cards.title: Asset expiry
cards.body: Time-bound licences, model and property release dates, brand-mark windows. Auto-alert N days before expiry; workflow to renew or retire. Orphaned expired assets are legal risk.

cards.title: AI for assets
cards.body: Auto-tagging by image recognition · auto alt-text and captions · text-to-image first drafts · variant generation (crops, sizes, formats) · semantic search ("confident young professionals in an office").

blocks.html: How quickly content moves from idea to published. The single most underrated metric in content programmes — and the one that decides whether the business ships campaigns weekly or quarterly.

items: Measure — cycle time per content type, throughput per week, time-in-stage per approval gate.
Common bottlenecks — approval gates without SLAs · translation queues · design rework loops · waiting on assets that don't exist yet.
Levers — templated component-based authoring · smaller faster approval loops · decoupled parallel authoring · automated translation first pass · AI-assisted drafting.

blocks.html: High content velocity is a competitive advantage. The brand that can ship a relevant campaign on a Friday afternoon will out-execute the brand that needs three weeks of approvals — regardless of which has the prettier creative.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Content
items: Is there a defined content model, and does every type fit it?
Is the IA sitemap maintained alongside the design system?
Is the source documented for every content type — including who provides and how?
Is the approval workflow named (people, SLAs, escalation) — not just understood?
Are tags and metadata enforced at upload, or applied retroactively?
Are asset expiries tracked, and what happens at expiry?
What is the current content velocity per type, and where's the bottleneck?
Where is AI assisting today, and where will it next?
